key_departures.impact: Retired unexpectedly at 27. Was the starting center and a key part of the offensive line. Created an immediate hole at center that the Bears moved quickly to fill via trade.

Traded to Buffalo with a 2026 fifth-round pick in exchange for a 2026 second-round pick. Was a top target and #1 WR over three seasons in Chicago. Removes a proven starter but Bears collected draft capital in return.
Released after 3 seasons as a full-time starter, saving $15M in cap space. Signed a 3yr/$36M deal with the Giants. Was a 100-tackle-per-season anchor at LB. Created an immediate need at the position.
Signed a 1yr/$9M deal with the Patriots. Pro Bowl-caliber veteran had been a multi-year starter in Chicago. Part of a complete safety room turnover alongside Brisker and Gardner-Johnson departures.
Signed a 1yr/$5.5M deal with the Steelers. Second-round pick in 2022 who started all 17 games in 2025 with 93 tackles, 1 INT, and 8 PDs. Leaves a starter-caliber void opposite Coby Bryant.

Signed a 1-year deal with the Bills. Had a standout season for the Bears in 2025. Along with Byard and Brisker, completes a total safety room exodus — the Bears enter free agency with essentially zero experienced safeties on roster.

Left for Atlanta after one season in Chicago. Finished with 30 catches for 315 yards. Was a depth/rotational piece but contributed meaningfully when starters were hurt. Departure deepens the WR hole created by the DJ Moore trade.
Signed a 1yr/$5.5M deal with the Jets. Had a career year in 2025 with 5 interceptions and 11 pass deflections, earned Pro Bowl nod. Significant departure for Chicago's secondary.
Signed a 1yr/$4M deal with the Texans. Was a solid rotational lineman stout against the run but never became a consistent pass rusher. Modest depth loss at EDGE.
Released to clear approximately $2M in cap space. Was a depth/special teams piece. Low impact departure.
Depth TE departed. Low impact given Cole Kmet still anchors the position.
Nose tackle hitting free agency, to be replaced by Neville Gallimore signing. Bears anticipated this departure and acted quickly.

signings.analysis: Acquired via trade (2027 fifth-round pick to NE) after Drew Dalman's surprise retirement. Bradbury will carry a $5.7M cap hit in 2026, cheaper than Dalman. He is a known, durable veteran center who played alongside a young QB in Drake Maye and is familiar to HC Ben Johnson from the NFC North. However, he is a clear downgrade from Dalman in terms of play quality and this is a one-year bridge solution, not a long-term fix. IOL remains a need for the future, though the starter hole for 2026 is plugged. Re-signings of Jones/McFadden provide additional depth.

The Bears' biggest splash of free agency, addressing the complete safety room exodus. Bryant is 26, Super Bowl champion with Seattle, and has made 7 interceptions over the past two seasons. With $25.75M guaranteed on a 3-year deal, this is a genuine investment, not a bridge. However, with Brisker, Byard, and Gardner-Johnson all gone, Bryant fills only one of the two starting safety spots. The Bears still need a second legitimate safety starter — Elijah Hicks and Cam Lewis provide depth/versatility but neither is a proven starter. S remains a partially addressed need. Draft must be considered.

Direct replacement for Tremaine Edmunds at $10M AAV vs. Edmunds' $15M, saving the Bears $5M annually. Bush had a career-best 2025 season — 125 tackles, 3 INTs, 2 sacks, and was the only player with multiple pick-sixes. Three years with $21M guaranteed signals genuine starter investment. PFF graded the signing 'Very Good.' However, TJ Edwards is rehabbing a fractured fibula and Noah Sewell is also injured, so depth behind Bush and D'Marco Jackson is thin. LB is partially addressed — starter secured, but depth/developmental needs remain, potentially in the draft.

Bears' first signing of free agency, replacing the departing Andrew Billings. Gallimore had a solid 2025 — 3.5 sacks, 38 tackles, 70.2 PFF pass-rush grade in a rotational role. The 2-year/$12M deal (with only $5M guaranteed) signals a rotational/depth role behind Gervon Dexter and Grady Jarrett, not a starter. PFF graded this 'Below Average' relative to market. IDL remains a significant need — the Bears still need a higher-quality starter at the position and will likely address it in the draft.

One-year deal reuniting Raymond with HC Ben Johnson and assistant Antwaan Randle El from his Lions tenure. Raymond is primarily a return specialist (three return TDs in last four years) and a WR4/depth piece. He had 24 catches for 289 yards with Detroit in 2025. This does NOT solve the void left by trading DJ Moore — it provides a familiar slot/depth body and punt returner. WR remains a critical need, particularly for a dynamic playmaker opposite Rome Odunze and Luther Burden III.

Re-signed on a 1-year/$10M deal to compete for the starting LT spot with Ozzy Trapilo expected to miss most of 2026 recovering from a torn patellar tendon suffered in the Wild Card game. Jones started 44 games at LT over four seasons but struggled in 2025 after an ankle injury. The 1-year structure is a prove-it deal — not a long-term solution. Combined with the Jedrick Wills signing, the Bears have competition at LT but no secure long-term answer. OT remains a moderate need, especially for 2027 and beyond.

Re-signed on a 2-year/$5.5M deal (max $8M). Keenum functioned as a player-coach mentor for Caleb Williams in 2025 and the Bears retained his presence in the QB room. This is a backup/developmental-role signing with no impact on team needs at QB — Caleb Williams is the unquestioned starter.

Re-signed on a 2-year/$7.5M deal (max $10.5M). Jackson was originally claimed off waivers for his familiarity with DC Dennis Allen's system from his time in New Orleans. He led the team in special teams tackles in back-to-back seasons and provides LB depth behind Bush. Does not solve the starter issue on his own but is a quality depth complement alongside Bush.

Two-year deal for a 6-year veteran who has primarily been a reserve — 10 starts over the last two seasons in 76 career games. Lewis can play nickel, outside CB, and safety, providing versatility in a decimated secondary. His 2025 PFF grade of 66.0 is serviceable for depth. This does NOT address the CB need created by Nahshon Wright's departure — Lewis is depth/flexibility, not a replacement for a Pro Bowl-caliber starter. CB remains a significant need heading into the draft.

Veteran depth signing of a player DC Dennis Allen previously coached at his career-best in 2022 (3.5 sacks with the Saints). Street has 10.5 career sacks in 85 games — a jack-of-all-trades rotational lineman. This is widely viewed as the Dominique Robinson replacement. EDGE remains a major need; the Bears are building from the inside out and are expected to prioritize pass-rush impact via the draft.

A 2020 first-round pick (No. 10 overall) signed on a low-risk, 1-year prove-it deal. Wills has significant injury history — MCL tear in 2023, only 4 starts in 2024, sat out 2025 entirely to recover. PFF graded him at 55.5 with just 210 snaps in 2025 production. This is a high-upside, low-cost lottery ticket that provides competition at LT alongside Braxton Jones, Benedet, and Kiran Amegadjie with Ozzy Trapilo sidelined. Does not solve the LT need — OT remains a priority in the draft.

Restricted free agent re-signed to a 2-year deal worth $4.97M with $2.45M guaranteed and $1M in incentives. Hardy was primarily a special teams ace, leading the team in special teams tackles in back-to-back seasons, with occasional snaps as a defensive end and fullback. Retention of a core special teamer — does not address the pass-rush need at EDGE.

Re-signed on a veteran depth deal. Hicks was on the practice squad for most of 2025. His return is a numbers/depth move only and does not address the need for a second starting-caliber safety alongside Coby Bryant.
Interior OL depth re-sign on a minimum-level deal. McFadden provides positional flexibility and familiarity with the offense. No impact on IOL starter need.
Minimum-level re-sign on a 1-year/$1M deal. Benedet is a developmental OT and camp body who provides depth behind Jones, Wills, and Amegadjie. No meaningful impact on the starting LT competition.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Complete safety room overhaul — Byard, Brisker, and Gardner-Johnson all departed. Coby Bryant (3yr/$40M, $25.75M gtd) is a genuine investment at one spot, partially addressing the need. But Chicago still has no proven second starter. Elijah Hicks and Cam Lewis are depth bodies, not solutions. S drops from the most critical need to a tier 2 priority — still a legitimate early-round draft target.

Edmunds released and replaced by Devin Bush (3yr/$30M, $21M gtd) in a strong value signing ($5M cheaper AAV). Bush is a genuine starter investment coming off an elite 2025 season. D'Marco Jackson re-signed as a complement. However, TJ Edwards and Noah Sewell are both rehabbing significant late-season injuries and depth behind Bush/Jackson is thin. LB moves from critical to a tier 2 need — depth/developmental piece still warranted in the draft.

Dalman's surprise retirement created an urgent need, but the Bears quickly traded a 2027 fifth-rounder for Garrett Bradbury (1-year bridge at $5.7M cap hit). The 2026 starter spot is filled, McFadden re-signed for depth. However, Bradbury is a clear talent downgrade and is on a 1-year deal — IOL remains a long-term need for 2027+. Drops to tier 3 as an addressable mid-round draft target.

DJ Moore traded to Buffalo (with a 5th) for a 2nd-round pick, and Olamide Zaccheaus signed with Atlanta. Kalif Raymond (1yr/$5.1M) is a return specialist and WR4 replacement, not a No. 1 receiver. Rome Odunze and Luther Burden III step up in role but the team lost a proven veteran presence. WR escalates to tier 1 — the Bears need a dynamic playmaker opposite Odunze, either in the draft (where they now hold additional 2nd-round capital from the Moore trade) or free agency.

Nahshon Wright (Pro Bowl, 5 INTs in 2025) departed to the Jets on a 1yr/$5.5M deal. Cam Lewis signed (2yr/$6M) but is a reserve/depth piece, not a starting replacement. Jaylon Johnson (on roster, recovering from injury) is the only established starter. CB escalates to tier 1 — the secondary urgently needs a starting-caliber CB2/outside corner. This should be a high priority in the first two days of the draft.

Dominique Robinson left for Houston. Kentavius Street (depth) and Daniel Hardy (re-signed, special teams) do not address the starting pass-rusher need. Dayo Odeyingbo is returning from a torn Achilles and his 2026 effectiveness is highly uncertain. Montez Sweat remains elite but needs a legitimate second pass rusher opposite him. EDGE remains a tier 1 need — the Bears' biggest unaddressed position. Expected to be a top-three draft priority.

Neville Gallimore (2yr/$12M) partially addresses the void left by Billings, but is a rotational/depth piece behind Dexter and Jarrett — not a high-end starter. Grady Jarrett underperformed in 2025. The Bears still need a quality interior disruptor. IDL drops from critical to tier 2 — still a draft priority, potentially in rounds 2-3.

Ozzy Trapilo is out most of 2026 with a torn patellar tendon. Braxton Jones re-signed (1yr/$10M) as the likely starter; Jedrick Wills (1yr/$5M) adds a high-upside but injury-prone option; Benedet provides depth. No signing is long-term — all three are 1-year deals. OT drops to tier 2 because the Bears have a reasonable bridge solution for 2026, but a long-term franchise LT is still needed and should be targeted via the draft.

key_additions.headline: Super Bowl champion safety (7 INTs over 2 seasons) fills biggest positional void after total safety room exodus
Career-best 2025 (125 tackles, league-leading 2 pick-sixes) replaces Edmunds at $5M cheaper AAV
Veteran center bridges the Dalman retirement gap, familiar to HC Ben Johnson from NFC North
Rotational interior pass rusher (3.5 sacks, 70.2 PFF pass-rush grade in 2025) replaces Andrew Billings
Familiar LT retained as bridge starter while Ozzy Trapilo recovers from torn patellar tendon
Return specialist and WR4 reunites with HC Ben Johnson, replacing Duvernay role in return game
Versatile secondary depth piece (CB/nickel/safety) added to rebuild depleted defensive backfield
Former No. 10 overall pick adds high-upside LT competition after missing full 2025 season to injury

key_losses.headline: Top WR over three seasons in Chicago dealt for second-round pick; creates urgent receiver need
Three-year starter (900 career tackles) released to save $15M; replaced by Devin Bush
Surprise retirement at 27 forced Bears to quickly trade for Garrett Bradbury as bridge center
Pro Bowl safety departs as part of total secondary overhaul; third starting S lost in free agency
Four-year starter (52 games) returns to Pittsburgh; Bears still need a second safety starter
Pro Bowl alternate (5 INTs, 11 PDs in 2025) lost to Jets; CB2 vacancy is now a top draft priority
Standout 2025 season not enough to keep him; completes the Bears' complete safety room turnover

notes: Neville Gallimore contract: ESPN/Sun-Times reported $12M/2yr; Spotrac/247 Sports shows $10M/2yr with $5M gtd. Used $12M (Sun-Times sourcing) as primary figure. Kentavius Street contract terms not publicly disclosed; estimated at veteran minimum range (~$2.5M). Jedrick Wills contract terms not fully reported; PFF lists as 1yr/$5M. Cam Lewis contract: 247Sports confirmed 2yr/$6M with $2.75M gtd. D'Marco Jackson: Yahoo Sports confirmed 2yr/$7.5M with $3.625M gtd and $1.875M signing bonus. Braxton Jones: Aaron Wilson confirmed 1yr/$10M with $3M gtd.
